item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gosh darn that song is catchy!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the tarrare reference if it was meant to be one + 1

item.title: Junior Staff Post - WikiWalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Edit: timer expired, edits made

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

This is an article of the GoI Herman Fuller's Circus. Therefore, I propose linking to the GoI's hub.

Editing this:

SCP-2902-1 claims that it and SCP-2902-2 came from the GOI Herman Fuller's Circus of the Disquieting

To this:

SCP-2902-1 claims that it and SCP-2902-2 came from the GOI Herman Fuller's Circus of the Disquieting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love, love this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: spooked me good

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this thing, but there is one thing I noticed.

Right in the beginning of Mile's little number, it just suddenly shifts from one cat to two. He's whole on Nandin's head, jumps to the floor whole, and suddenly his skeleton is dancing without mentioning the separation. Not sure if that was intended or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think part of it is to "not grow attached to the specimens", but it's a rule that's been danced around in the past. Iris and Able come to mind. However, it is more common for the numbers to be abbreviated - for this example, if she doesn't want to say "Miles" it would make sense for her to say "talk about 2902-2", pronounced as "twenty-nine-oh-two".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was a great read. It was interesting seeing the Circus from a more "positive" perspective. (I mean, I'm fully aware that the Circus itself is usually a negative influence, but that part isn't as visible here.) Also, the Queen reference was a plus. (And there being a Queen reference does make sense as well.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The best part of this is imagining Dr. Chang corpsing through the whole performance. "Must not laugh, must not laugh, it's unprofessional, it is a bad idea to laugh, god dammit why did I have to get assigned to this guy must not laugh…"

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted for Lando Calrissian.
Oh, and the rest of the article was good too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just hate this trope because it's almost never established that the person being spoken to understands the designation. Just a "I would like to ask about SCP-10000-52" "Oh, uh, the fire-breathing cat?" exchange establishes that this has been gone over before and really takes the edge off this scenario.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If say the CI wanted an object and they knew its designation, they then captured a list of SCP objects at the same facility, a list with both the object they want and SCP-2902 on it, and 2902 escapes and the CI is able to talk to him, they now know where the object they want is. Even seemingly inconsequential details can be used to build actionable intelligence.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Being called by a number would remind some people of concentration camps, and other people would be alarmed: "these guys refuse to call me by my name, clearly they have their own agenda and are not very trustworthy".
And yes, being called SCP-2902 would allow one to deduce that there's an abbreviation for paranormal people, and that there's 2901 more of them around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
- say, if you're trying to establish an emotional connection to put a nervous humanoid skip at ease or something-

That should be the default case.

but as a general rule it makes sense to stick to numbers when possible

I strongly disagree, even if you discount the need to bulid rapport with the subject, there's still the issue of freely sharing classified intelligence with a contained anomaly (without it being a necessary exchange). Even the existance of the SCP system is probably a Level 1 secret.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not saying there aren't situations where it makes tactical sense to use a real name - say, if you're trying to establish an emotional connection to put a nervous humanoid skip at ease or something- or that there aren't people with a greater ability to compartmentalize their emotions, but as a general rule it makes sense to stick to numbers when possible. Here, for instance, the guy doesn't really care if they call him SCP-2902-1, and he has a career's worth of experience at working marks and ingratiating himself with tough audiences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: People in the real world interrogate other people, and use their real names. If the Foundation really has a problem with this they are using the wrong people. Just don't have people who are psychologically incapable of detachment conduct interviews in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To me, its a "maintaining detachment" thing:
If you give a rock a name, you've automatically made it exponentially easier to become emotionally attached to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't really make a lot of sense, for reasons I feel like I have now utterly belabored. I wish we'd just stop doing it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've noticed that too. I guess it's a narrative thing? I've definitely had drafts where reviewers told me the interviewer was too stiff. (but then again I've also got stuff like SCP-2255 who is a cloud who serves a flower goddess and SCP-1338 who is a little tree child)

Maybe it's supposed to indicate detachment, or to emphasize the fact that the interviewer has control of the conversation, and the interviewee needs to ask for info if they need it or don't understand it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great article, I'm not sure it's the right place to discuss this, but there's a Foundation trope used here that grinds my gears.

Chang: I'd like to talk about SCP-2902-2 today.

What's the deal with "Foundation personnel must be ultra-professional and only reference SCP objects by their numbers"? A sign of greater professionalism would be talking to people using the language they understand. This guy really doesn't give a damn about Foundation nomenclature for example, so why not call a spade a spade, and a cat a cat?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just for grins and giggles, I did a reverse Google Image Search on the picture of the dude, and, well… this is what I found. Make of it what you will, SCP wiki. Do with this gift what you will.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
*claps*

This is one of the most charming skips I've read in a while.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh, that changes thing… I had assumed they had just placed him inside of the skin of a taxidermized (is that a word?) cat that had died of unrelated reasons :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Got it, thanks!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As a general rule, sapient scips are Euclid by default.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not a matter of danger, it's a matter of breach capability. He could escape if you drop your guard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought this was great, but the only thing I'm wondering is why this is Euclid and not Safe? There doesn't seem to be anything dangerous at all here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was half expecting an "and then a skeleton popped out" joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I loved this. I've probably said this a million times before, but I love SCPs that manage to be entertaining without being dangerous or scary. This one was very charming. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like I've been exposed to a memetic thrill agent.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I may have read it too quickly and got dizzy with all the SCP-2902-X's bones and skins and bodies being referenced all the time…

…But there was a point in the last Interview where it all was flowing so rhythmically that I wasn't even reading who did what. I was imaging the whole picture with the music and lyrics and dancing skeletons and animated cat furs and I just love this shit take all my upvotes god damn.

<3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks! :D

If you're interested in other stuff I've written this is my author page :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This…is absolutely brilliant! Good show, good show (no pun intended)! I wonder what else you may have done..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I just really like the surrealistic horror on this site and the jokes have always muddled that for me. As a writer, I admit I got butthurt and let that show (prob shouldn't be sipping jim beam and going on here tbh). I apologize. It's unbecoming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ayy, I'm just gonna leave it at that and not keep forcing the issue. Nothing to see here mates.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The era of grimderp on this site died back in 2011. We do a lot more than horror, and have done so for ages now. Since the beginning, honestly. Some of our oldest bits are very dumb jokes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's nothing particularly interesting, thought-provoking, well-written, or all that heartwarming about this.

Two questions:

- what are the specific things about this draft that you consider show a lack of the qualities listed above?

- why do you think others have upvoted it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's nothing particularly interesting, thought-provoking, well-written, or all that heartwarming about this.

Of course that's my opinion.

Only reason I posted this is that you got guys like Kalinin who wouldn't upvote your article if you hooked up a car battery to their balls… immediately upvoting a zany indian guy who loves his cat XDDD.

I like this site. I like how hard it is to make a successful article, makes you feel like you earned it. But yeah, I am butthurt, cause from the outside looking in this is just ridiculous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You've almost answered your own question, grasshopper- there are several things that this skip has that less successful articles lack. They aren't things which can be firmly defined or distilled to a template, but they can be learned. A tale is all in the telling; look for the forest in the trees. ^_^

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nevermind. It was bad judgment. Carry on.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No doubt about it, which is why I'm posting it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your opinion, while certainly as valid as anyone else's, is clearly in the minority.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it's honestly not that good. seems like all that effort in trying to come up with novel horror in the year of our lord 2015 should just be redirected into channeling the reddit main page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because well-written, heartwarming fun is still a thing in this world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll never understand how the main list can be such a meat grinder but a zany indian singing zany songs about his zany cat can explode like this. Makes a nilla just wanna bow out of the game tbh

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
(reads)

…

(smiles)

I like! (nods) Definite upvote. Chang maintains her professionalism throughout — though I should imagine she couldn't help cracking a smile, especially during Miles' section — and the subject is very well-realized throughout. Good job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, for the catches in the formatting! Regarding your other points:

+ Show
- Hide

Why "as of January 29, 2008"?

Honestly just a date; no particular significance.

Not an enormous issue, but I do struggle a bit to accept that there are kittens (of a breed that comes from North America) wandering the streets of Indian towns.

I probably should elaborate more on this one. From what I researched, cats are not very popular in India. In Miles' song, when he said "thrown out of my home", I imagined a family from another country bringing a pet cat, and having it chased out by the people running wherever they were staying.

Is this intended to say that Nandin knew that Miles wouldn't make any noise (which is how it reads), or that he was hoping that Miles wouldn't make any noise?

Hmmmm… I meant to mean it as hoped; I can try and change the wording a bit.

Later, she pushes the bars out to leave. How did she get in?

In style.

This doesn't seem to be the purpose of the interview - the topic raised by Ms Chang is -2's origin, and there is no information given about what happened after the Circus abandoned the pair.

Whoops, that was left over from an earlier draft. Fixed.

I didn't understand this - is the idea that the skin was cast off? There must be a way to make that scan: "My old skin cast off, and my bones removed"?

And just to be too pedantic, if Miles was a kitten when he first met Nandin, and only three months passed before he died, how are his bones big enough to fill an adult Maine Coon?

Yeah, the old Miles was skinned because one, the skin was all bloodied up from being run over (which would detract from performances) and two, clowns just do weird stuff in regards to magic/voodoo/whatever they do.

Oh, the maine coon wasn't an adult; it was another kitten and with the procedures done, Miles' bones would grow along with the new body. Think extreme bone fusing; the maine coon wasn't hurt per se, but it had its skeleton and mind taken over by Miles'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They sound non-clinical. For example, "are capable of completely separating their skeletal structures from their bodies" sounds better for the first one.

"While their anomalous properties are active/manifest" would be better for the second, and "devoid of other matter" or something would be a better way to phrase the third.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked this, but I'm not sure I loved it as much as others have. Nandin is quite a well-drawn character (I particularly like some of his Indian idiom in the first interview, which was subtle enough to not feel shoehorned in) and I agree that the Circus feels more real here than other GoIs typically do. I think my objection is that as an origin story, it reads a little cliched (poor Indian boy, domineering uncle, magical realism saves the day, everybody dance!). Perhaps I was just spoiled by the heightened expectations generated by previous comments. Or perhaps I'm just a curmudgeon.

+ Also I have some proofing comments
Collapse like an empty skin

SCP-2902-1 is, as of January 29, 2008, a 32-year old human male of Indian descent

Why "as of January 29, 2008"?

Let me begin the tale of Naandin Chakrabarti

Should that be "Nandin"?

At least, that's what my uncle told me

In the second interview, you use capitalised "Uncle" throughout - should this be made consistent?

I found him during my last recent escape, in the bushes lining the street outside Uncle's house. He was still a kitten, then.

Not an enormous issue, but I do struggle a bit to accept that there are kittens (of a breed that comes from North America) wandering the streets of Indian towns.

I remember the only thing I was thinking of as he punched me was that Miles wouldn't make any noise.

Is this intended to say that Nandin knew that Miles wouldn't make any noise (which is how it reads), or that he was hoping that Miles wouldn't make any noise?

When I woke up again it was still night, and there was a girl in my room.

Later, she pushes the bars out to leave. How did she get in?

Foreword: Interview conducted to learn information about SCP-2902-1 and 2 post-abandonment by the Circus of Disquieting.

This doesn't seem to be the purpose of the interview - the topic raised by Ms Chang is -2's origin, and there is no information given about what happened after the Circus abandoned the pair.

… And we're gonna put a smile
On you face

"On your face"?

SCP-2902-1 claps hands loudly and shake head; skeleton falls out

Should that be "pulls head"? That has been the trigger for the skeleton falling out previously.

Bring in the Clowns! Bring in the Clowns!**

Should those last two asterisks be deleted?

My old skin was cast, and my bones removed,
And stuffed into the body of a maine coon anew

I didn't understand this - is the idea that the skin was cast off? There must be a way to make that scan: "My old skin cast off, and my bones removed"?

And just to be too pedantic, if Miles was a kitten when he first met Nandin, and only three months passed before he died, how are his bones big enough to fill an adult Maine Coon?

[SCP-2902-1, in normal voice.)

I think that first square bracket should be an opening parenthesis.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't see huge issues with these phrases…?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay yeah holy fuck I love this.

I love this a lot. We need to make a recording of the song.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are some tone issues ("have their skeletons outside of their bodies", "during the time their skeletons are outside", "blank space",) but those should be easy to fix.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the anomaly is the trained cat, right? That just breaks suspension of disbelief too much, downvoted.

I was thoroughly charmed by this before I even read the interviews. What I'm feeling right now is precisely what one should feel after reading a "Happy-but-not-super-feelsy" story. Also, this was a great way to do a talking animal: ventriloquism.

Also, going to point out the fact that Circus of the Disquieting members not just being weirdos, but performers, rings of realism (relative realism, admittedly) that has been consistent throughout. In fact, one thing about the Circus that's more realistic than the other GOIs is the fact that Circus activity (meaning performances) can and mostly will be dismissed by the public as neat tricks, which means the Foundation doesn't need to hand amnestics out like candy, which only adds another layer of cool. I can dig this, definitely.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think I've ever seen a skip's backstory told in song before. This hits all the right notes, pun unintended.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the most magical skips I've read on the site. I'm so in love with this in many ways that I'm not sure how to describe it. I'll find some better words to use for this at some point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Omnious…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You'll have a chance to go at this subject to your heart's content soon enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you, Kalinin. This post made my day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Utterly fantastic. Tennouttaten. Hunnerdouttahunnerd.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in my estimation, have yet to contribute positively to a piece.

I can think of several that I can't see working at all without the GOC/Sarkism/Mechane-Broken God/Daevas/Alaggada/The Factory. I don't know if this is a useful line of discussion, but if it is, I will happily follow into a spin-off thread!
